---
title: "Notes"
subtitle: "The weekly design-and-evidence spine"
page-type: orientation
toc: true
---

The notes are the backbone of the course. There is one note per week, and reading the current week's note
before class — and again after — is the single most reliable way to keep up. Every note follows the same shape,
so once you learn to read one, you can read them all.

## How to read a week

Each weekly note is built from the same parts, in the same order. Knowing the anatomy lets you skim for what you
need and study with intent:

- **The week question.** A single design question that the week exists to answer. Hold it in mind as you read;
  everything else is in service of it.
- **Concept development.** The core ideas, built up in a few short sections from intuition toward a precise
  statement about *what a design can claim*. This is the part to read slowly — and where the design, the
  mechanism (sampling? assignment?), and the threats are made explicit.
- **Worked examples.** Each idea is worked on the recurring campus world — the **Focus experiment**, the
  **tutoring-center observational study**, or the **study-habits survey** — with the design set up, the
  computation shown, and the result interpreted in sentences — plus one *transfer* example in a fresh context,
  so you see the idea move.
- **A common mistake.** The design error students most often make on this topic — confusing sampling with
  assignment, reading association as causation, adjusting for the wrong variable — named plainly so you can
  watch for it in your own work.
- **Ungraded self-checks.** A few low-stakes practice prompts to test yourself. These are *self-check only* —
  no points, no submission.
- **Reading pointer.** Where to read more: the relevant *Introduction to Modern Statistics* topic (and, for the
  randomization and sampling weeks, *ModernDive*; for the causal-diagram weeks, the optional *Causal Inference:
  What If*), with the reminder that these notes are the course's own synthesis.
- **Evidence and verification status.** An honest note that the numbers in the example studies are drafted and
  synthetic, pending review — this is a draft course site.
- **Looking ahead.** A sentence or two connecting this week to the next, so the design arc stays visible.

Keep the [design glossary](../resources/design-glossary.qmd) and the
[study-design reference](../resources/design-reference.qmd) open alongside the notes.

## The four parts

The fifteen weeks fall into four parts. Each part has a job, and the weeks within it build on one another.

**Part I — Questions, measurement & validity.** What a statistical question is, how we measure, the signature
distinction between sampling and assignment, and the threats a claim must survive.

1. [Week 1 — Statistical questions & units of analysis](week-01-statistical-questions-and-units-of-analysis.qmd)
2. [Week 2 — Measurement & operational definitions](week-02-measurement-variables-and-operational-definitions.qmd)
3. [Week 3 — Random sampling vs random assignment](week-03-random-sampling-vs-random-assignment.qmd)
4. [Week 4 — Bias, confounding & validity](week-04-bias-confounding-and-validity.qmd)

**Part II — Designed experiments.** Randomized comparisons and the refinements that sharpen and structure them.

1. [Week 5 — Completely randomized experiments](week-05-completely-randomized-experiments.qmd)
2. [Week 6 — Blocking & paired designs](week-06-blocking-and-paired-designs.qmd)
3. [Week 7 — Factorial experiments (midterm)](week-07-factorial-experiments-and-midterm.qmd)
4. [Week 8 — Interactions in designed studies](week-08-interactions-in-designed-studies.qmd)

**Part III — Observational & causal evidence.** Studies without random assignment, where confounding lurks and
causal diagrams keep the reasoning honest.

1. [Week 9 — Observational studies](week-09-observational-studies.qmd)
2. [Week 10 — Causal diagrams & backdoor reasoning](week-10-causal-diagrams-and-backdoor-reasoning.qmd)

**Part IV — Surveys, sampling & synthesis.** Survey sampling, missing data, study critique, and a closing
synthesis.

1. [Week 11 — Surveys & sampling frames](week-11-surveys-and-sampling-frames.qmd)
2. [Week 12 — Stratified & cluster sampling](week-12-stratified-and-cluster-sampling.qmd)
3. [Week 13 — Missing data & nonresponse](week-13-missing-data-and-nonresponse.qmd)
4. [Week 14 — Study critique & design-memo workshop](week-14-study-critique-and-design-memo-workshop.qmd)
5. [Week 15 — Final design memo & review](week-15-final-design-memo-and-review.qmd)

## Public vs. graded

These notes and the practice in them are **public and ungraded** — study material only. No graded prompts,
answer keys, rubrics, point values, or due dates appear on this site. Graded design checkpoints, quizzes,
design memos and homework, applied design labs, the midterm, the final design project, and the final exam live
in **Blackboard (the LMS)**, which is authoritative for due dates, submissions, and grades. If this page and
Blackboard ever disagree, follow Blackboard.
